options: (A) To hit the board as hard as possible without thinking
(B) To focus on a point beyond the board rather than the board itself
(C) To wait until his hands stopped shaking completely
(D) To ask for a third attempt if needed
questions.explanation: 文中提到教練提醒他要專注在木板之外的一點，而非盯著木板本身，相信自己的技巧而非恐懼，故選 B。
optionsZh: 不假思索地用盡全力擊打木板
專注在木板之外的一點，而不是盯著木板本身
等到雙手完全停止顫抖再出手
如有需要就要求第三次機會

title: The Rise of Community Fridges
passage:
In cities around the world, an increasingly common sight on street corners is the community fridge, a simple refrigerator placed in a public location where anyone can leave extra food or take items they need, entirely free of charge and without requiring any explanation or paperwork. These fridges are typically maintained by local volunteers rather than any government agency or business.

The concept behind community fridges is straightforward but powerful in addressing food waste and hunger at the same time. Restaurants, grocery stores, and individual households often have surplus food that would otherwise be thrown away, and rather than discarding perfectly good items, they can simply place them in the nearest community fridge for someone else to use. Meanwhile, people facing financial difficulty can access fresh food discreetly, without the stigma sometimes associated with visiting a traditional food bank during limited operating hours.

Running a community fridge does require ongoing effort, since volunteers must regularly clean the refrigerator, check that food remains safe to eat, and ensure electricity continues running smoothly at the location. Despite these challenges, the number of community fridges has grown rapidly in recent years, particularly in neighborhoods where access to affordable, fresh food remains limited. Supporters argue that these small refrigerators represent a simple, community-driven solution to two problems that often go unnoticed until they affect someone directly.

questions.question: According to the passage, what two problems do community fridges try to solve at the same time?
options: (A) Traffic congestion and pollution
(B) Food waste and hunger
(C) Unemployment and housing shortages
(D) Electricity costs and water shortages
questions.explanation: 文中提到社區冰箱能同時解決食物浪費以及糧食不足的問題，故選 B。
optionsZh: 交通壅塞與空氣汙染
食物浪費與糧食不足
失業與住房短缺
電費成本與缺水問題

title: The Substitute Coach
passage:
Our regular soccer coach broke his leg two weeks before the championship tournament, leaving our team without proper guidance at the worst possible moment. The school quickly arranged a substitute coach, a quiet young man fresh out of college who had never coached a competitive youth team before, and most of us assumed the tournament was already lost.

Our new coach spent the first practice mostly observing rather than instructing, watching how each player moved and communicated during scrimmages before saying much of anything. Several teammates complained that he seemed unprepared, but by the second practice, he began making small, specific suggestions tailored to individual players rather than repeating generic drills our old coach had used for years. He noticed, for example, that our usual midfielder played much better when positioned closer to the goal, a change our previous coach had never considered trying.

By tournament day, our team played with a confidence and chemistry that surprised even ourselves, ultimately reaching the semifinals for the first time in school history. After our final match, several players thanked him directly, admitting they had doubted him unfairly at the beginning. He simply smiled and said that coaching was never really about years of experience, but about paying close enough attention to notice what a team genuinely needed.

questions.question: What can be inferred about the substitute coach's coaching style?
options: (A) He relied entirely on generic drills without adjusting to players
(B) He carefully observed players and made specific adjustments based on their strengths
(C) He ignored player feedback throughout the season
(D) He refused to change any strategies from the previous coach
questions.explanation: 文中提到他先仔細觀察球員，再針對個別球員提出具體調整，例如改變中場球員的站位，故選 B。
optionsZh: 他完全依賴一般性訓練，不因球員調整
他仔細觀察球員，並根據他們的優勢做出具體調整
他整個賽季都忽視球員的意見
他拒絕改變前任教練的任何策略

title: Why Owls Can Turn Their Heads So Far
passage:
Owls are famous for their ability to rotate their heads much farther than humans or most other animals, sometimes turning nearly two hundred seventy degrees in either direction, an ability that has fascinated scientists and casual observers alike for many years. This remarkable flexibility exists because owls face a unique physical limitation that most other birds and mammals do not share.

Unlike humans, whose eyes can shift and roll within their sockets to look in different directions, owl eyes are fixed firmly in place, unable to move independently at all. This design allows owls to gather more light and see more clearly at night, an essential advantage for hunting after dark, but it also means owls must move their entire head simply to look sideways or behind them. To compensate for this limitation, owls evolved specialized neck structures, including extra vertebrae and unusually flexible blood vessels that prevent blood flow from being cut off during extreme head rotation.

Without these specialized adaptations, rotating the head so dramatically would likely damage blood vessels and restrict blood flow to the brain, potentially causing the owl to lose consciousness. Scientists studying owl anatomy have even used these discoveries to inspire safer designs for medical equipment, including catheters intended to bend without collapsing. This unusual adaptation demonstrates how solving one biological limitation can lead to remarkably creative evolutionary solutions.

questions.question: Why do owls need to turn their heads so far compared to other animals?
options: (A) Because their eyes are fixed and cannot move independently
(B) Because their necks are naturally weaker than other birds
(C) Because they have no ears to detect direction
(D) Because they cannot fly forward easily
questions.explanation: 文中提到貓頭鷹的眼睛固定不能轉動，因此必須轉動整個頭部才能看向側面或後方，故選 A。
optionsZh: 因為牠們的眼睛固定，無法自行轉動
因為牠們的脖子天生比其他鳥類更脆弱
因為牠們沒有耳朵可以辨別方向
因為牠們無法輕易向前飛行

title: The Handmade Birthday Card
passage:
With only a few dollars left in my savings after buying school supplies, I could not afford a proper birthday gift for my best friend's fifteenth birthday, so I decided to make her a card by hand instead, secretly worried that it would look cheap and thoughtless compared to gifts from her other friends. I spent nearly three evenings cutting colored paper, drawing small illustrations, and carefully writing memories we had shared since elementary school.

Each page of the card described a different memory, from the time we got hopelessly lost during a school field trip to the ridiculous inside jokes that still made us laugh years later. My handwriting was messy in places, and one of my drawings looked more like a confused blob than the cat it was supposed to represent, but I refused to start over, worried I would run out of time before her party.

When she finally opened the card at her birthday party, surrounded by wrapped gifts from other guests, she went completely silent for several long seconds before hugging me tightly, tears forming in her eyes. She later told me that out of everything she received that day, my handmade card was the gift she kept displayed on her bookshelf months afterward. I learned that effort and thought could matter far more than anything bought from a store.

questions.question: What is the main idea of this passage?
options: (A) Handmade gifts always look messy and thoughtless
(B) A thoughtful handmade gift can mean more than an expensive store-bought one
(C) The narrator regretted not buying a proper gift
(D) Friendship depends entirely on the value of gifts exchanged
questions.explanation: 全文說明手作卡片雖不完美，卻讓朋友深受感動並長期珍藏，顯示用心的禮物可能比昂貴的商品更有意義，故選 B。
optionsZh: 手工禮物總是顯得凌亂又不用心
用心製作的手工禮物可能比昂貴的商品更有意義
敘述者後悔沒有買一份像樣的禮物
友誼完全取決於交換禮物的價值

title: The Science of Static Electricity
passage:
Almost everyone has experienced a small electric shock after touching a metal doorknob or noticed hair standing up after pulling off a wool sweater, both common examples of static electricity in everyday life. This phenomenon occurs when electrons, tiny negatively charged particles, transfer from one object to another through friction, leaving one surface with extra electrons and the other with a shortage.

When two different materials rub against each other, such as your shoes against a carpet or a balloon against your hair, electrons can jump from one surface to the other. This creates an imbalance of electrical charge, with one object becoming negatively charged and the other becoming positively charged. Since opposite charges naturally attract each other, this imbalance can cause noticeable effects, including hair standing on end or a small spark jumping between your finger and a metal object once you touch it.

Static electricity tends to be far more noticeable during dry winter months compared to humid summer weather, because moisture in the air actually helps electrical charges dissipate more quickly rather than building up on surfaces. This explains why static shocks feel far more common and stronger during cold, dry seasons. While usually harmless, understanding static electricity has also helped engineers design safer equipment for handling sensitive electronics, which can be damaged by even small, sudden static discharges.

questions.question: According to the passage, why is static electricity more noticeable in winter?
options: (A) Because cold air contains more electrons naturally
(B) Because dry air allows electrical charges to build up rather than dissipate
(C) Because people wear more metal jewelry in winter
(D) Because winter clothing is always made of wool
questions.explanation: 文中提到潮濕空氣有助於電荷更快消散，而乾燥的冬季空氣則讓電荷更容易累積，故選 B。
optionsZh: 因為冷空氣天生含有更多電子
因為乾燥空氣讓電荷容易累積而非消散
因為人們在冬天配戴更多金屬飾品
因為冬季衣物永遠都是羊毛材質

title: The Lemonade Stand
passage:
When I was eleven years old, I decided to set up a lemonade stand outside our house to save money for a bicycle I desperately wanted, convinced that customers would line up eagerly the moment I opened for business. My first Saturday morning was a complete disaster, with almost no one stopping by except my own neighbors, who politely bought a cup out of obvious sympathy rather than genuine thirst.

Discouraged but not quite ready to give up, I asked my older cousin for advice, and she suggested moving my stand closer to the busy park entrance a few blocks away instead of our quiet residential street where few people ever walked past. She also recommended making a colorful sign and offering a slightly lower price than the convenience store nearby, small changes that felt almost too simple to actually matter.

The following Saturday, positioned near the park entrance with my new sign, I sold nearly triple the amount from my first attempt, and by the end of summer, I had saved enough money to buy the bicycle along with a helmet. Looking back, that small lemonade stand taught me more about paying attention to what customers actually wanted than any single school lesson ever had, a lesson that stuck with me long after the bicycle eventually wore out.

questions.question: What change helped the narrator sell more lemonade the second Saturday?
options: (A) She raised her prices significantly
(B) She moved her stand near the busy park entrance and made a colorful sign
(C) She gave away free samples to everyone passing by
(D) She asked her parents to help sell the lemonade
questions.explanation: 文中提到表姊建議她把攤位移到人潮較多的公園入口附近，並製作醒目的招牌，這些改變讓她第二週賣出更多檸檬水，故選 B。
optionsZh: 她大幅提高了售價
她把攤位移到人潮較多的公園入口，並做了醒目招牌
她開始免費贈送樣品給路過的人
她請父母幫忙賣檸檬水

title: How Mountains Are Formed
passage:
Mountains might appear permanent and unchanging, standing exactly as they have for millions of years, but they are actually the result of powerful geological forces that continue shaping the Earth's surface even today, though usually far too slowly for humans to notice within a single lifetime. Most mountains form through the movement of massive plates that make up the Earth's outer crust, which are constantly shifting, colliding, and sliding past one another over enormous spans of time.

When two of these plates collide head-on, neither one able to slide beneath the other easily, the tremendous pressure forces the land upward, gradually creating towering mountain ranges over millions of years. The Himalayas, home to the world's tallest peaks, formed this way when the plate carrying India slowly collided with the much larger Asian plate, a collision that actually continues today, meaning these mountains are technically still growing taller extremely slowly each year.

Not all mountains form through collision alone. Some result from volcanic activity, building up gradually as repeated eruptions deposit layer after layer of cooled lava and ash. Others form through massive cracks in the Earth's crust, where certain sections of land rise while neighboring sections sink lower, creating dramatic differences in elevation over relatively short geological distances. Regardless of exactly how they form, mountains remain living proof of the powerful, ongoing forces shaping our planet beneath our feet.

questions.question: According to the passage, why are the Himalayas still technically growing taller?
options: (A) Because volcanic eruptions continue adding new lava layers
(B) Because the collision between the Indian and Asian plates is still ongoing
(C) Because erosion has stopped affecting the mountains
(D) Because scientists are adding measurements incorrectly
questions.explanation: 文中提到印度板塊與亞洲板塊的碰撞至今仍在持續進行，因此喜馬拉雅山理論上仍在極緩慢地增高，故選 B。
optionsZh: 因為火山噴發持續增加新的熔岩層
因為印度板塊與亞洲板塊的碰撞至今仍在持續
因為侵蝕作用已經停止影響這些山脈
因為科學家的測量方式有誤

title: The School Play Backstage
passage:
I auditioned for our school's spring play hoping for a small speaking role, but instead I was assigned to work backstage handling props and costume changes, a position I initially considered a disappointing consolation prize rather than a real part of the production. For the first few rehearsals, I quietly resented spending hours in the dark wings while other students received applause under the bright stage lights.

During the second week of rehearsals, our lead actress suddenly lost her voice completely during a particularly demanding scene, and the entire cast froze in confused silence, unsure how to continue without stopping the rehearsal entirely. Having memorized nearly every line from listening backstage for weeks, I quietly fed her the missing lines from just offstage, allowing the rehearsal to continue smoothly without the director even noticing the brief interruption.

Afterward, our director pulled me aside and thanked me, admitting she had never realized how closely backstage crew members needed to follow the entire script just to do their jobs properly. On opening night, watching from the wings as costume changes happened flawlessly and props appeared exactly on cue, I finally understood that the performance the audience applauded depended entirely on work happening in the shadows where nobody could see it. I left that production with a completely different appreciation for every role involved in bringing a play to life.

questions.question: What can be inferred about the narrator's view of backstage work by the end of the passage?
options: (A) He still considered it unimportant compared to acting
(B) He gained a new appreciation for the importance of backstage roles
(C) He quit the production after the incident
(D) He decided backstage work was easier than acting
questions.explanation: 文末提到他終於了解觀眾鼓掌的演出，完全仰賴幕後看不見的工作，因而對每個角色都有全新的體會，故選 B。
optionsZh: 他仍然認為幕後工作比演戲不重要
他對幕後工作的重要性有了全新的體會
他在事件發生後退出了這場製作
他認為幕後工作比演戲更簡單

title: The Benefits of Walking to School
passage:
Decades ago, walking or cycling to school was the normal way most children traveled each morning, but today far fewer students walk regularly, with many instead being driven by car even for relatively short distances. Health experts and researchers have become increasingly concerned about this shift, pointing to numerous benefits that regular walking provides beyond simply getting from one place to another.

Studies have shown that children who walk to school tend to arrive more alert and focused, likely because physical activity increases blood flow to the brain and helps reduce morning restlessness before classes even begin. Regular walking also contributes to healthier body weight and stronger cardiovascular fitness over time, benefits that become increasingly important as childhood obesity rates continue rising in many countries around the world.

Beyond physical health, walking to school with friends or family members offers valuable opportunities for conversation and social connection that a quiet car ride typically does not provide in the same way. Some schools have responded to these benefits by organizing supervised walking groups, sometimes called walking school buses, where an adult volunteer walks a fixed route each morning, picking up small groups of students along the way. These programs address safety concerns that many parents raise about traffic while still preserving the physical and social advantages of walking together as a group.

questions.question: What is a 'walking school bus' as described in the passage?
options: (A) A special bus that only stops near schools
(B) A supervised group of students walking a fixed route with an adult volunteer
(C) A digital app that tracks students walking to school
(D) A new type of electric vehicle for schools
questions.explanation: 文中提到「walking school buses」是由成年志工帶領，沿著固定路線一路接學生的步行團體，故選 B。
optionsZh: 一種只在學校附近停靠的特殊公車
由成年志工帶領、沿固定路線步行的學生團體
一款追蹤學生步行上學的數位應用程式
一種給學校使用的新型電動車

title: The Violin Lesson
passage:
My parents enrolled me in violin lessons when I was eight years old, mostly because my older sister had already quit piano after only three months, and they hoped I might show more dedication toward a different instrument. For the first two years, I dreaded every single weekly lesson, convinced that the screeching sounds coming from my violin would never improve no matter how much I practiced.

Everything changed slightly during my third year, when my teacher assigned a piece that was noticeably more difficult than anything I had previously attempted, warning me honestly that most students needed several months of daily practice before playing it smoothly. Rather than feeling discouraged, something about the challenge sparked genuine determination for the first time, and I began practicing an extra thirty minutes each evening without my parents needing to remind me at all.

After nearly four months of steady practice, I performed the piece during our studio's spring recital, and although I made two small mistakes partway through, I finished the piece feeling prouder than I had ever felt about anything violin-related before that moment. My teacher told me afterward that watching a student move from dreading practice to seeking out challenges was the most rewarding part of teaching. I still play violin today, though now entirely because I genuinely want to, not because my parents once signed me up.

(B) A difficult piece sparked genuine determination in her
(C) Her sister returned to playing piano alongside her
(D) Her teacher offered a reward for daily practice

title: The Science of Ocean Currents
passage:
Beneath the surface of the world's oceans flows a vast, interconnected system of moving water known as ocean currents, powerful streams that circulate constantly around the globe, driven by a combination of wind patterns, differences in water temperature, and the rotation of the Earth itself. Some currents flow along the surface, easily influenced by wind, while others move slowly through the deep ocean, driven mainly by differences in water density.

These currents play an enormous role in regulating global climate patterns, often in ways that surprise people unfamiliar with ocean science. Warm currents originating near the equator carry heat toward polar regions, while cold currents move in the opposite direction, helping distribute temperature more evenly across the planet than would otherwise be possible. Certain coastal regions owe their surprisingly mild climates almost entirely to nearby warm currents passing along their shores, even at latitudes that would typically be much colder without this influence.

Ocean currents also affect marine life significantly, transporting nutrients that support fish populations and other sea creatures across vast distances. Fishing communities have relied on this knowledge for generations, understanding that certain currents reliably bring nutrient-rich water that attracts large numbers of fish during specific seasons. As global temperatures continue changing, scientists are closely monitoring whether shifting ocean currents might disrupt these long-established patterns, potentially affecting weather, fishing industries, and coastal communities around the world in ways not yet fully understood.

questions.question: According to the passage, how do ocean currents affect global climate?
options: (A) They prevent all temperature changes on Earth
(B) Warm and cold currents help distribute heat more evenly across the planet
(C) They only affect temperatures in polar regions
(D) They have no measurable effect on climate at all
